Clarity: Describes the specific behavior not the person's character
Uses a concrete example from a real situation
States clearly what needs to change
Avoids generalizations like 'you always' or 'you never'
Timing & delivery: Given close to when the event happened
Delivered privately not in front of others
Checks in on how the feedback landed before ending
Actionability: Focuses on what the person can actually change
Ends with agreement on next steps

overlaps: Describes the specific behavior not the person's character
Focuses on what the person can actually change

leftItems: Checks whether the message landed before moving on
Adapts the level of detail to what the listener already knows
Repeats key points multiple times to make sure they land
Keeps the conversation flowing to prevent any silence
Prepares detailed talking points before every important conversation

partA.topic: What makes a strong project manager?

Communication: Tells the team what winning looks like
Delivery: Manages scope to avoid scope creep

overlaps: Defines what success looks like
Tells the team what winning looks like
Updates stakeholders regularly
Keeps everyone informed on progress

leftItems: Does what they say they will do
Flags issues to the right people before they become problems
Tells people what they want to hear to keep things smooth
Admits mistakes only when directly asked about them
Changes position when pressured

Integrity: Tells the truth even when it is uncomfortable

Structure: Opens with the key message not background
Covers only what the audience needs to know
Follows a logical flow from problem to solution
Ends with a clear call to action
Delivery: Speaks with confidence and eye contact
Paces the talk so key points land
Controls the rhythm so the audience can follow
Audience fit: Removes anything irrelevant to this specific audience
Anticipates questions and addresses them early
Leaves one clear takeaway the audience remembers

overlaps: Covers only what the audience needs to know
Removes anything irrelevant to this specific audience
Paces the talk so key points land
Controls the rhythm so the audience can follow

leftItems: Everyone knows who owns the final call on key decisions
Psychological safety to speak up
People are selected primarily for cultural fit over capability
No conflict ever
Everyone is aligned before any decision is made

partA.topic: Why do projects go off track?

People: Key stakeholder wasn't looped in early
Team didn't raise risks to the manager
Important person was left out of decisions
Process: Scope wasn't defined clearly at the start
No clear owner for key decisions
Timeline was too aggressive given resources
Nobody was accountable for the final call
Execution: Deadlines were missed and no one flagged it
Testing was skipped under time pressure

overlaps: Key stakeholder wasn't looped in early
Important person was left out of decisions
No clear owner for key decisions
Nobody was accountable for the final call
Progress wasn't tracked or escalated
Deadlines were missed and no one flagged it

partB.topic: What does executive presence look like?
leftItems: Speaks with conviction even under uncertainty
Reads the room and adjusts in real time
Commands the room by speaking first and setting the direction
Maintains composure by keeping emotions off the table
Defers to subject matter experts rather than inserting their own view

Communication: Gets to the point without over-explaining
Frames ideas in terms of impact not activity
Composure: Stays calm when things go wrong
Awareness: Knows what matters to who is in the room

partA.topic: What makes someone ready for promotion?

Performance: Consistently delivers results beyond what the role demands
Does the job without needing to be told what to do
Operates above the bar set for their current level
Gets things done in ambiguous situations without hand-holding
Leadership: Others seek them out for input before making decisions
Creates alignment across people with different priorities
Takes initiative on things that fall outside their job description
Moves the group forward when direction is unclear
Readiness: Is already solving the problems of the job above
Understands how influence actually works at the next level
Has demonstrated they can operate without the scaffolding of their current role
Knows what matters to the people who will decide on the promotion

overlaps: Consistently delivers results beyond what the role demands
Operates above the bar set for their current level
Takes initiative on things that fall outside their job description
Moves the group forward when direction is unclear
Is already solving the problems of the job above
Has demonstrated they can operate without the scaffolding of their current role

leftItems: Considers second-order effects not just the immediate outcome
Is made at the right time — not too early or too late
Draws on the decision-maker's past experience rather than reopening the analysis
Is revisited on a regular schedule to confirm it is still the right call
Comes from whoever has the most relevant expertise in the room

Thinking: Uses available data without waiting for perfect information
Process: Involves the right people at the right level
Outcome: Can be explained and defended clearly
Is revisable if new information emerges

partA.topic: What goes into a successful apology?

Acknowledgment: Names the specific behavior that caused harm
Accepts responsibility without adding context as an excuse
States clearly what went wrong without deflecting
Repair: Asks what the other person needs to feel okay
Makes a specific commitment to change
Follows through on what was promised
Timing: Given as soon as the impact is understood
Not rushed when the other person is not yet ready to hear it
Does not wait for the other person to raise it first

overlaps: Given as soon as the impact is understood
Does not wait for the other person to raise it first

partB.topic: What helps when you feel overwhelmed?
leftItems: Say out loud exactly what is making this moment feel unmanageable
Pick the one thing on your list that does not actually need to happen today and move it
Stay busy so you do not have time to worry
Add more structure and systems right away
Push through until everything is done

Slow down: Take a breath before responding to anything
Step away from the situation for a short time
Get clear: Write down exactly what is on your mind
Identify the one thing that matters most right now
Act small: Choose one thing to do next
Complete something small to build momentum

partA.topic: What makes a close friendship last?

Trust: Keeps what you share between you
Tells you hard truths instead of comfortable agreement
Does not speak for you to others without checking
Does not judge your past against who you are becoming
Presence: Shows up when things get difficult
Does not need an occasion to reach out
Reaches out without waiting for you to ask first
Remembers what you are going through right now
Growth: Makes space for who you are becoming
Challenges your thinking without dismissing your view
Is not threatened by your success or how you have changed

overlaps: Does not need an occasion to reach out
Reaches out without waiting for you to ask first
Tells you hard truths instead of comfortable agreement
Challenges your thinking without dismissing your view

partB.topic: What makes it easier to focus deeply?
leftItems: All notifications are off and out of reach
You know exactly what done looks like for this session
You feel completely motivated before you begin
You have cleared everything else off your list first
You have given yourself a generous time block so there is no pressure

Environment: Your phone is in another room
The space signals that it is time to work
Mindset: You have already decided what you are doing — no choices to make mid-session
You are not fighting urgency from other open tasks
Momentum: You start with something small to build traction
The last session ended with clear notes on where to pick up

partA.topic: What does emotional intelligence look like in practice?

Self-awareness: Can name what they are actually feeling beyond stressed or fine
Notices when a reaction is bigger than the situation warrants
Recognizes which situations trigger them before they are already inside one
Tracks the gap between what they intended and the impact it had
Regulation: Responds from a choice rather than a reflex
Can sit with discomfort without needing to resolve it immediately
Does not need the situation to change in order to feel okay
Empathy: Holds their own position while genuinely hearing someone else's
Does not need to win an argument to feel secure
Can receive hard feedback without shutting down or deflecting

overlaps: Recognizes which situations trigger them before they are already inside one
Responds from a choice rather than a reflex
Can sit with discomfort without needing to resolve it immediately
Does not need the situation to change in order to feel okay
Holds their own position while genuinely hearing someone else's
Does not need to win an argument to feel secure

partB.topic: What separates people who achieve long-term goals from those who don't?
leftItems: Returns to the process after falling off rather than waiting to restart
Their environment makes the right behavior the path of least resistance
Has extraordinarily high willpower
Never misses a day once they commit
Relies on motivation and momentum to keep going

Mindset: Sees a setback as data rather than failure
Measures progress against where they started not where they want to be
Systems: Has a default routine that runs without motivation
Removes decisions that drain willpower before they happen
Identity: Defines themselves by the process rather than the outcome
Acts as though they are already the person who does this consistently
